source.title: The basal ganglia: focused selection and inhibition of competing motor programs

confidence_rationale: Foundational statement of the focused-selection hypothesis: the BG do not generate movement; they broadly inhibit competing motor mechanisms while focally disinhibiting the desired one. This is essentially a single-mechanism account -- one selection process with surround inhibition, not three separate gating loops. Mink 1996 has anchored the unified-selection view in motor-systems neuroscience for almost three decades, and the supporting electrophysiology (Boraud 2000, MPTP I/A ratios) is genuinely empirical, not just theoretical. Confidence is slightly below Redgrave 1999 because Mink's hypothesis is narrower (motor programs specifically) and so the mapping to Q-019's full three-domain claim (sensorium / thought / commitment) requires more transfer.
failure_signatures: If three anatomically distinct gating loops each implement their own selection computation, then the focal-disinhibition / surround-inhibition signature should NOT be a unified property of GPi/SNr output across loops -- it should differ per loop. Mink 1996's evidence and the Boraud 2000 MPTP follow-up suggest the signature IS unified, which weakens strict three-gate.

If sensorium, thought, and commitment loops each have separate arbitration, surround inhibition should be loop-internal (motor-loop GPi cells inhibit competing motor programs but not competing thoughts). Mink 1996 doesn't directly test this but the focused-selection framework as developed assumes a single inhibition pattern across BG output -- the three-gate model would predict otherwise.

Mink's account is silent on whether selection is for motor PROGRAMS specifically or for any competing input -- if it is motor-only then the three-gate framing might still hold for non-motor (sensorium, thought) loops. This is the principal weakening risk for using Mink 1996 against three-gate.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The basal ganglia do not generate movements. When voluntary movement is generated by cortical and cerebellar mechanisms, the BG act broadly to inhibit competing motor mechanisms that would otherwise interfere, while simultaneously removing inhibition focally from the desired motor mechanism. Inability to inhibit competing programs explains the bradykinesia, abnormal postures, and involuntary movements of BG disease.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-019 asks whether BG implement three anatomically distinct gating loops or one unified selector. Mink 1996 is a foundational statement of the unified-selector view -- one selection process implemented as surround-inhibit / focal-disinhibit on a shared GPi/SNr output stage. For REE this means option (A) is not just a theoretical possibility (Redgrave 1999) but has empirical anchoring in single-unit electrophysiology and clinical lesion data; the basal ganglia output stage shows the unified signature regardless of which input channel is driving the selection.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Mink 1996 was framed for MOTOR program selection. Extending the focused-selection account to thought-loop or sensorium-loop selection requires assumptions Mink does not test. So Mink 1996 weakens Q-019 option (B) for the motor commitment loop strongly, but its weakening of the sensorium and thought loops is by analogy. The three-gate model could in principle be correct for non-motor loops while Mink's account is correct for the motor loop. That hybrid possibility is what Q-019 was registered to arbitrate.
mapping.source_context: Long-form review in Progress in Neurobiology, 1996; integrates monkey single-unit recording, lesion data, and clinical neurology. Anchors a major line of motor-systems thinking; influential for two decades. Context: motor program selection in voluntary movement.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high (foundational, integrates extensive empirical literature). Mapping fidelity moderate-high -- Mink directly argues for unified motor selection but Q-019 spans non-motor domains too. Transfer risk moderate -- extending focused-selection across loop types is exactly what Q-019 wants to test. The MPTP follow-up (Boraud 2000, PMID 10712496) provides empirical anchoring for Mink's hypothesis.
